blocks.content: Lena sees them coming from thirty yards away.

She has been on the bench near the quieter rides, not reading the book she brought, not looking at her phone — just sitting with the afternoon, keeping her place in the field, waiting. She has been doing this, in one form or another, for three years.
She rises before they reach her.

She reads Cassandra's face in the first second — the redness, the particular quality of stillness that follows something enormous moving through a person, the way her eyes are different. Not worse. Opened.
Cassandra lets go of Ethan's hand and walks to Lena.

She doesn't say anything. She walks into her arms and holds on — not the composed hug, not the managing hug — the real one. The kind that says: you were right. You were right and it's done and I'm okay.

Lena holds her with the fierce, whole love of someone who has been here for every version of this, who sat in the waiting room at the cardiologist and learned to give Cassandra space and support in the same motion. Who has been carrying her own weight of this quietly, for years, without making it about herself.
She doesn't ask. She just holds on.
